text: I've wanted to make a video like this for a while on how I do a laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon marks the planned incision sites on the patient's abdomen prior to making the incisions for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon decides to proceed with a vertical incision, marking the skin before making the cut.
The surgeon uses a scalpel to make a vertical incision, cutting down almost to the fascia layer.
The surgeon uses S retractors to sweep the adipose tissue away from the fascia at the midline, exposing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon demonstrates careful handling of the scalpel blade, ensuring it does not catch on the skin during the incision process.
It's fairly rare that I use Coker clamps in patients.
The surgeon cuts the fascia under direct vision, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses two hemostats to grasp the thin peritoneum and opens it sharply, revealing the underlying structures.
The surgeon uses an S-retractor to sweep open the thin peritoneum, revealing underlying structures without the need for sharp dissection.
The surgeon places two stay sutures from side to side across the surgical field, preparing them to be tied down for closure at the end of the procedure.
The surgeon places an S-retractor into the fascial opening, providing exposure to the underlying structures.
The surgeon uses a second retractor to expose the fascia, providing a clear view of the tissue layer for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon places the first stitch across the exposed area, preparing for closure after the peritoneum has been opened.
The surgeon uses a hemostat to hold the two suture tails together, preparing for further manipulation or closure.
The surgeon places an S-retractor back into the surgical field, effectively exposing the fascia for further manipulation.
An S-retractor is placed into the fascial opening, providing exposure for the surgical field.
The surgeon places the second stitch across the exposed fascia, using retractors to maintain clear visibility of the surgical field.
An S retractor is placed back into the opening, effectively exposing the fascia for further surgical access.
The surgeon places the second stitch within the fascial opening, using a hemostat to hold the stitch tails together.
At the end of the case I'll place one stitch in between those two and if necessary I'll place even more sutures than that.
The surgeon places an additional retractor inside the abdominal cavity, using it as a shoehorn to aid in exposure.
The surgeon demonstrates the adjustable collar on the Hassan Trocar, setting it to the appropriate length and locking it in place within the abdominal cavity.
Here I adjusted it a little bit more.
The surgeon secures the collar of the Hassan Trocar in place using stay sutures and connects the insufflation tubing to facilitate abdominal cavity access.
For me, where you place the trocars and their trajectories are extremely important.
The surgeon places the epigastric port high on the abdomen, just below the lower edge, directing it towards the cystic duct for optimal access during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon adjusts the trajectory of the epigastric port, directing it laterally to avoid the falciform ligament during the cholecystectomy procedure.
We then turned the table to the patient's left and did not need to use any reverse Tranelenburg.
I'll place my left hand very close to the right, usually five to six centimeters away.
If it gets further away, it's not the right angle, and then you have to operate with your left arm up in the air, which is not very comfortable.
The surgeon positions the instruments close to the costal margin, maintaining proximity to the anatomical boundary during the procedure.
In this patient, I could have been just a little bit closer to the costal margin.
The surgeon demonstrates the positioning of the trocar, indicating that it cannot be directed medially during the procedure.
It has to go in a lateral direction away from you.
Otherwise your trocars end up too close together on the inside.
The surgeon places the lateral trocar in the abdominal wall, ensuring minimal torquing to maintain stability during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon positions the trocar in a skive fashion, angling it slightly to optimize placement within the abdominal wall.
The surgeon demonstrates the placement of a trocar with its tip moving from a posterior to an anterior trajectory, resembling an upside-down orientation.
The surgeon demonstrates the desired angle for inserting the five-in-one trocar, showing its trajectory as it enters the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon positions the fundus grasper at an optimal angle, facilitated by the strategic placement of the trocars.
The surgeon adjusts the placement of the trocar, achieving a near-desired angle for optimal access during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a five-millimeter, thirty-degree laparoscopic scope to visualize the surgical field throughout the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon picks up the gallbladder and hands it to the grasper inserted through the lateral trocar, effectively retracting the gallbladder for better exposure.
The surgeon demonstrates the angle of the grasper used to retract the gallbladder, ensuring it is positioned correctly through the lateral trocar.
The surgeon uses a grasper to lift the gallbladder, providing a clear view of the surrounding anatomical structures.
The tips of the surgical instrument are seen digging into the gallbladder, with a risk of puncturing its surface.
The surgeon lifts the fundus of the gallbladder and uses a lateral grasper inserted through the lateral trocar to retract the gallbladder.
The surgeon adjusts their grip on the gallbladder, revealing a small capsule or tear on the liver surface due to an adhesion.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to cauterize and partially remove an adhesion between the gallbladder and liver, reducing the visible capsule or tear on the liver surface.
The surgeon lifts the gallbladder to visualize adhesions around its neck, assessing the extent of tissue attachment and exposure.
The surgeon uses their left hand to grasp the periduodenal fat and begins freeing the duodenum from the gallbladder, carefully separating the two structures.

The surgeon retracts the gallbladder to remove adhesions but encounters insufficient tension, prompting the reapplication of traction on the duodenum to effectively separate additional adhesions from the gallbladder surface.
The surgeon begins freeing up the gallbladder neck, carefully separating it from surrounding adhesions and tissue.
The surgeon manipulates the gallbladder back and forth to identify a suitable plane for dissection, preparing to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon further releases the duodenum from the gallbladder, continuing to separate the two structures by addressing remaining adhesions.
The surgeon adjusts the grip on the gallbladder to increase retraction, improving the exposure of the gallbladder for further dissection.
The surgeon freezes the frame to demonstrate the direction of retraction required for the cystic duct, highlighting the orientation and tension needed for optimal exposure.
The surgeon positions an instrument in line with the cystic duct, aligning it for optimal manipulation during the cholecystectomy procedure.
Here I could have had my instrument a little closer to the rib cage.
You don't want your left hand too low in the abdomen because then you can't provide the proper retraction angle.
This makes exposure much more difficult.
The surgeon visualizes the cystic artery and begins clearing adipose tissue from the gallbladder neck to better expose the cystic duct and cystic artery.
The surgeon manipulates the gallbladder back and forth to assess the optimal side for dissection, then proceeds to carefully free the lateral peritoneal attachments.
The surgeon uses a hook to dissect into the proper plane, moving it back and forth to separate tissues, followed by using a quarter to divide the tissues further.
The surgeon uses a hook to ensure it is positioned across small vessels encountered during the dissection, preparing to divide them safely.
The gallbladder is now freed from its attachments, and the surgeon positions the left hand at an optimal angle for further manipulation.
The surgeon works on the medial aspect of the gallbladder, carefully freeing up the peritoneal attachments using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon uses a hook instrument to finely dissect the tissues around the gallbladder, carefully separating them from surrounding structures.
Sometimes I use a Maryland dissector, but for a case like this, that was not necessary.
The surgeon clears fat and tissues from the anterior and posterior sides of the cystic duct, using precise dissection techniques to expose the duct.
The surgeon uses a hook to gently push the cystic artery, revealing the dissection plane for further surgical progress.
The surgeon begins the cystic artery mobilization technique, manipulating the cystic artery to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon frees the cystic artery from the gallbladder wall, exposing the backside of the cystic duct and the undersurface of the gallbladder.
The surgeon places a hook behind the cystic artery to create a window, then pulls back on the connective tissue behind it to cauterize and divide the tissue.
The surgeon attempts to develop a window underneath the gallbladder neck, adjusting the position for better exposure of the area behind the gallbladder.
The surgeon continues dissection on the backside of the gallbladder, focusing on the area around the cystic artery and duct.
The surgeon divides additional attachments and flips the gallbladder back towards the medial aspect, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon successfully creates a window underneath the gallbladder, providing clear visualization of the cystic duct and artery.
The surgeon uses a hook to enlarge the window behind the cystic artery, pulling back connective tissue to cauterize and divide it.
The surgeon uses Grasper jaws to carefully expose and enlarge the window underneath the gallbladder, improving visibility of the surgical field.
The surgeon assesses the anatomy, noting that the cystic plate is not fully cleared, which prevents achieving a true critical view of safety.
The surgeon continues to free the gallbladder by dividing lateral peritoneal attachments and working on the medial aspect, aiming to achieve a true critical view of safety.
This video is run at two times speed and minimally edited.
The surgeon uses hook cautery to further free attachments on the medial aspect of the gallbladder, with the cystic duct appearing slightly prominent.
At this point we began discussing whether or not the true critical view of safety had been achieved.
The surgeon continues to free the gallbladder from the cystic plate, inadvertently creating a small opening in the gallbladder, resulting in bile drainage.

The surgeon carefully uses a hook cautery to free the gallbladder from the liver, ensuring precise dissection to separate the gallbladder from its attachments.
The decompressed gallbladder is visibly easier to manipulate, allowing for smoother dissection and freeing of attachments using hook cautery.
The surgeon carefully dissects the artery away from the gallbladder wall, further separating these structures.
The surgeon uses a five millimeter clip applier, likely to secure structures such as the cystic duct or artery during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon places a clip on the artery and slides another clip onto the neck of the gallbladder, securing these structures during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to remove spilled bile from the surgical field, ensuring a clear view of the gallbladder and surrounding structures.
I do routine intraoperative clangograms.
The surgeon uses scissors to perform a cystic ductotomy, making an incision in the cystic duct.
Bile is visibly returning, indicating the presence of bile flow in the surgical field.
The surgeon gently milks the cystic duct to ensure there are no stones present within it.
The surgeon inserts a five French open-ended ureteral catheter into the cystic duct and secures it with a clangogram clamp, preparing for an intraoperative cholangiogram.
The intraoperative cholangiogram shows normal opacification of the biliary tree, with no visible filling defects and normal contrast runoff into the duodenum.
The surgeon prepares to clip the cystic duct following the removal of the ureteral catheter, ensuring the duct is ready for ligation.
The surgeon secures the cystic duct, preparing it for clipping as part of the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon applies clips across the cystic duct, ensuring complete coverage of the duct with the clips.
The surgeon positions the cystic duct perpendicular to the clipper plier, preparing for secure clipping.
The surgeon places two clips securely across the cystic duct, ensuring proper closure.
The surgeon applies three clips across the cystic duct, ensuring secure closure in this patient.
The surgeon places two additional clips on the cystic artery, ensuring secure ligation of the vessel.
The surgeon observes the alignment of two clips on the cystic duct, noting that they are not as well aligned as the three clips previously placed.
The surgeon cuts the cystic duct and cystic artery after ensuring proper alignment of the clips on these structures.
The surgeon frees the gallbladder from the gallbladder fossa, carefully separating it from the underlying liver tissue.
There's not really any secret or magic to this part of the case.
The surgeon uses the left hand to pull the gallbladder away from the liver, creating tension to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon grasps the gallbladder on its lateral aspect, preparing to work on this area during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses improved retraction techniques to enhance visibility and access to the gallbladder, facilitating its separation from the gallbladder fossa.
The surgeon adjusts the traction by pulling the gallbladder laterally with the left hand to improve exposure, while working underneath the gallbladder to release it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies a clear dissection plane beneath the gallbladder and begins using the heel of the hook instrument to separate the tissue layers.
The surgeon identifies a small vessel within the gallbladder fossa, visible during the dissection process.
The surgeon releases the traction on the gallbladder and repositions the fundus grasper onto the true fundus of the gallbladder, adjusting the grip for improved manipulation.
The surgeon grasps the gallbladder on its lateral aspect and frees up some lateral attachments, improving exposure and mobility of the gallbladder.
The surgeon returns to the medial aspect of the gallbladder, freeing peritoneal attachments to improve exposure of a small vessel in the gallbladder fossa.

The surgeon places their left hand underneath the gallbladder to stabilize it, freeing attachments to better visualize the posterior vessel. The hook is used to carefully dissect the vessel from surrounding tissue.
You could argue that there's probably just a small vessel that can be cauterized, but I get concerned about a potential biliary communication.
The surgeon carefully dissects the lateral attachments of the gallbladder, revealing a small vessel. Using hook cautery, a window is created around the vessel for better visualization.
The surgeon applies a clip to a small vessel located close to the liver, securing it to prevent bleeding during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon attempts to cauterize a small vessel near the gallbladder using a hook cautery, taking care not to dislodge the previously placed clip on the vessel.

The surgeon uses the heel of the hook cautery instrument to carefully dissect around a small vessel near the gallbladder, ensuring that the energy does not conduct down to the previously applied clip.
The surgeon frees the remaining attachments holding the gallbladder, allowing it to be flipped over the liver, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon inspects the gallbladder fossa, observing that the area is hemostatic with no visible bleeding.
The surgical field shows no active bile leakage, although some bile is visible in the area.
The surgeon irrigates the surgical field to remove bile, ensuring there is no visible leakage from the gallbladder or surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator, carefully avoiding the small side holes to prevent surgical clips from getting caught during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to touch two specific areas, ensuring hemostasis, before completely releasing the gallbladder from the gallbladder fossa.
The surgeon moves the laparoscopic scope to the epigastric trocar and places the gallbladder into a specimen bag for removal.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to clean the abdominal cavity, removing any residual bile and ensuring a clear surgical field.
